title: CC Exercises — APIs & Integration
description: Hands-on exercises covering OCC API exploration, custom endpoint creation, populators/converters, and integration pattern design.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Explore OCC with Postman & the Fields Parameter
sections.content:
Objective

Build a comprehensive Postman collection for the OCC API, master OAuth authentication, and understand the fields parameter for response control.

Steps

Part A — OAuth Authentication

  1. In Postman, create a request to the OAuth token endpoint: POST /authorizationserver/oauth/token.
  2. Get a Client Credentials token (anonymous access): set grant_type=client_credentials, client_id and client_secret. Save the returned access_token.
  3. Get a Resource Owner Password token (user access): set grant_type=password, username, password, client_id, client_secret. Save this token separately.
  4. Configure a Postman environment variable {{token}} and use it as Bearer token in all subsequent requests.

Part B — Product API with Fields

  1. GET /occ/v2/{{baseSite}}/products/search?query=camera — observe the full response structure.
  2. Repeat with ?query=camera&fields=BASIC — note the reduced response.
  3. Repeat with ?query=camera&fields=products(code,name,price(FULL)),pagination — note you get exactly the fields you requested.
  4. GET a single product with ?fields=FULL and document the complete response structure: name, description, images, price, stock, categories, classifications.

Part C — Cart & Checkout Flow

  1. Create an anonymous cart: POST /users/anonymous/carts with the user token. Note the cart GUID.
  2. Add a product: POST /users/anonymous/carts/{{guid}}/entries with product code and quantity.
  3. Get the cart: GET /users/anonymous/carts/{{guid}}?fields=FULL. Study the entries, totals, and applied promotions.
  4. For a logged-in user: repeat with /users/current/carts and your user token. Note how current resolves to the authenticated user.

Validation

Your Postman collection should have 10+ requests with working authentication. The fields parameter demonstrably controls response size. Document the percentage reduction between FULL and BASIC responses for a product search.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Build a Custom OCC Endpoint
sections.content:
Objective

Create a custom REST endpoint that exposes LoyaltyCard data through OCC, following the Controller → Facade → Service → DAO layered architecture.

Steps

  1. Create the WsDTO: LoyaltyCardWsDTO with fields: cardNumber (String), points (Integer), tier (String), customerUid (String).
  2. Create a Populator: LoyaltyCardPopulator implementing Populator<LoyaltyCardModel, LoyaltyCardWsDTO>. Map all fields from the model to the DTO.
  3. Create a Facade: LoyaltyFacade with method getCardForCurrentUser() that calls the LoyaltyService, converts the result using DataMapper, and returns the DTO.
  4. Create the Controller:
    @RestController
@RequestMapping("/occ/v2/{baseSiteId}/loyalty")
public class LoyaltyController {
    @Resource private LoyaltyFacade loyaltyFacade;
    @Resource private DataMapper dataMapper;

    @GetMapping("/card")
    @ApiOperation(value = "Get loyalty card for current user")
    public LoyaltyCardWsDTO getCard(
        @RequestParam(defaultValue = "DEFAULT") String fields) {
        return loyaltyFacade.getCardForCurrentUser(fields);
    }
}
  5. Configure field set level mappings for LoyaltyCardWsDTO: BASIC = cardNumber,tier; DEFAULT = cardNumber,points,tier; FULL = cardNumber,points,tier,customerUid.
  6. Add Swagger annotations (@Api, @ApiOperation, @ApiParam) to make the endpoint appear in Swagger UI.
  7. Register all beans in Spring configuration. Build and deploy.

Validation

Call your endpoint with Postman: GET /occ/v2/{{baseSite}}/loyalty/card?fields=FULL with a user token. Verify the response contains all loyalty data. Test with fields=BASIC and verify only cardNumber and tier are returned. Check Swagger UI and verify the endpoint documentation appears.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Additive Populator for Standard Endpoints
sections.content:
Objective

Extend the standard product detail OCC response with a custom field using the additive populator pattern — without modifying any Spartacus or OCC code.

Steps

  1. Ensure the Product type has a custom attribute (e.g. warrantyMonths from previous exercises).
  2. Create ProductWarrantyPopulator implementing Populator<ProductModel, ProductWsDTO>. In the populate() method, read warrantyMonths from the model and set it on the DTO. (You may need to extend ProductWsDTO or use the generic properties mechanism.)
  3. Register the populator as a Spring bean and add it to the product converter's modifiable populator list:
    <bean parent="modifyPopulatorList">
  <property name="list" ref="productConverter" />
  <property name="add" ref="productWarrantyPopulator" />
</bean>
  4. Build and restart.
  5. Call the OCC product detail endpoint: GET /occ/v2/{{baseSite}}/products/{{productCode}}?fields=FULL.
  6. Verify the warranty field appears in the response alongside all standard product fields.

Bonus Challenge

Create a second populator that adds the loyalty tier of the current user to the UserWsDTO response. Test by calling /occ/v2/{{baseSite}}/users/current?fields=FULL.

Validation

The standard product endpoint returns warranty data without any modification to the commercewebservices extension. The additive pattern is confirmed: your populator contributes alongside the existing populators, and removing it restores the original response.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Hot Folder CSV Import
sections.content:
Objective

Set up a Hot Folder that watches for CSV files and automatically imports product data — simulating an ERP-to-Commerce Cloud batch integration.

Steps

  1. Prepare the CSV file: Create a file product_prices.csv with columns: productCode, currency, price, minQty. Add 10 rows with price data for your existing products.
  2. Configure the Hot Folder: In your extension, set up a Hot Folder configuration that:
    • Watches a specific directory (e.g. /path/to/hotfolder/prices/).
    • Uses a CSV-to-ImpEx transformer with a header mapping that maps CSV columns to PriceRow ImpEx fields.
    • Routes successfully processed files to an archive directory and failed files to an error directory.
  3. Create the mapping: Define the ImpEx header that the transformer generates: INSERT_UPDATE PriceRow; product(code, $catalogVersion)[unique=true]; currency(isocode)[unique=true]; price; minqtd; ...
  4. Drop the file: Place the CSV file in the watched directory.
  5. Monitor: Watch the server logs for the Hot Folder pickup, transformation, and import. Check the archive/error directories to see where the file ended up.
  6. Verify: Run a FlexibleSearch to confirm the PriceRows were created with the correct values.

Bonus Challenge

Introduce an error in one CSV row (e.g. a product code that doesn't exist). Verify that the file goes to the error directory and the error log identifies the problematic row. Fix the row and re-drop the file.

Validation

The Hot Folder automatically detects and processes the CSV file. PriceRows are created in the database. Successful files go to archive, failed files go to error. The round-trip from CSV drop to database should happen without any manual intervention.
